title: THE BLACK DOOR
text: THE BLACK DOOR by Anthony J. Rapino V anessa wanted to be convinced. I could tell by her eyes. “Will it hurt?” The corner of her mouth twitched. A smile’s teasing presence. “That’s the whole point.” I called Gregory over. “Check.” “Nothing to eat, Sam?” He stood thin and tall, hanging over the table like a bent tree. We’d only had drinks. Bourbon for me. Amaretto sour for Vanessa. “Not tonight.” I ate at The Black Door most weeks, and not because of the name. An old acquaintance, Benny Dickson, owned the place. I’d get comped every so often, but that’s not why I always ate there either. “You promised me dinner.” Vanessa leaned forward and her body did all kinds of interesting things as she pressed against the tabletop. If you guessed I patronized The Black Door because of the food, you guessed wrong. Most dishes were too something . Too greasy, too salty, too fishy. Gregory watched Vanessa wiggle in her seat. He was my waiter. Mine as in Benny hired him for me. Mine as in he was the only person allowed to wait on me. He offered, “How about two usuals to go? I can have it out before you finish your next round.” “I didn’t order—” The bartender appeared and placed our drinks on the table. “As I said. Before you finish your next round.” The man was a pro, but Gregory was also not the reason for my recurring attendance at The Black Door. Vanessa pushed her lush red hair behind her ears. “What’s the usual?” She leaned back. Her summer dress danced across umber skin. “Too spicy.” Our table, my regular table, sat alone in the far corner of the dark dining area. Some would say too dark. I’d heard customers complain that they couldn’t read the menu. That they couldn’t see the food. I’d argue you didn’t want to see the food. “I like spicy.” “Then you’re in luck.” Vanessa peered around the room lazily. “What will we use? If I decide to go home with you, I mean.” “Haven’t you already decided?” Her smile showed plainly now. “You’ve promised a lot. Can you deliver?” I drank my bourbon deliberately and completely. The warmth spread through me. “That depends entirely on you.” Gregory approached and placed a takeout bag on the table. “Your food.” “Almost didn’t make it.” I waggled my empty glass. He dropped another bag onto the table. “Your necessities.” The plastic bag contained a surprising weight and even more surprising contents. This was why I returned to The Black Door time after time, not that I had a choice. “Thank him for me,” I said, meaning it for perhaps the first time since Benny saved my life. As we stood to leave, Gregory placed a skeletal hand on my shoulder. He squeezed too hard and whispered. “At your earliest convenience, Mr. Dickson would like a word.” I buttoned my suit jacket. “Tomorrow.” “Morning.” “Something I should know?” He released his grip and brushed his white shirt smooth. “Bring what you owe.” I took Vanessa’s arm. “Fair enough. Tomorrow morning then.” # I met Benny Dickson in college. Really, I should say we met at college. Neither of us attended Eskuatep, but we were both drawn to the university by the cove, as so many others were. The private institution was home to the country’s strictest admission process, highest price tag, and best doctoral program. Being rich wasn’t enough. Students also had to be geniuses. I wasn’t there for a medical degree. I was an explorer. A journeyman. At least those were the romantic things I told myself. There were stories about the college and the surrounding town. This was before the Internet, when urban legends ran rampant and verification wasn’t always as easy as a few keystrokes. So I left my job as an apprentice fishmonger—one job of dozens from which I’d absconded over the years for similar reasons—and hitchhiked to Delaware. To the university by the cove, where stone walkways led the uninitiated astray, winding baffling routes that to nowhere. When I arrived in Delhyme, the cool sea air hit me with a salty tang. Dusk had fallen, and in a small town like this, few were on the street. The local businesses were closing for the evening, and no one gave me a second look as I vagabonded up the road. So much stonework. Buildings, pillars, statues, cobblestone roads. White rocks weathered with tiny craters or smoothed by rain. Darker boulders acting as natural fences between road and lawn. To find the furniture inside every house was made of rock wouldn’t have surprised me. Creaking shutters from a nearby building aroused my attention. I turned in time to see a light extinguish, and I could feel eyes in the darkness watching. All around me, windows were opened to black and whatever lay beyond. # “ This is your place?” Vanessa’s tone sounded the same as all the others. When I brought women home for the rites, they always expected some dank hovel adorned with animal bones and laboratory equipment. Or maybe newspaper-lined windows and the faint smell of cat urine soaked into filthy carpeting. “It is. Do you like it?” When confronted with my brick townhouse in The Willows, they invariably were surprised. We ascended the staircase to the second floor, all hard wood, marble, and stainless steel. Vanessa gazed around the open floor plan from kitchen to living room, the approval plain on her face. “So you’re, like…rich?” I placed the bags on the kitchen counter. “Comfortable.” She narrowed her eyes. “You’re not at all what I expected from your profile.” “What did you expect?” I worked the knot loose on the plastic bag. “A depraved degenerate.” Her smile suggested she still thought I was. “Whatever gave you that idea?” A clear, gallon-sized plastic container sat inside the bag. I lifted it out. Vanessa watched with limited interest as she removed something from her own bag. She tilted the Bowie knife, catching glints from the rail lighting. “Just in case you don’t have your own tools.” Her teeth showed white and sinister. “We have options, but any blade will do.” I draped my jacket over a chair and retreated to a steamer trunk. I undid the cast iron latches and carefully opened the top, revealing a set of sanitized surgical tools and heavy-duty, shoulder length rubber gloves. “Holy shit.” Vanessa traced a finger across my neck, sending a shiver through me. “I’m so fucking turned on right now.” # The waterway which skirted the edge of campus was called Forgotten Cove. The location was famous for two things. One was its relative proximity to Eskuatep University. The second was for being the site of countless missing persons. From what I could tell, investigations never got far. Had Silvia Tull walked naked and wailing into Forgotten Cove as a thick mist swallowed her from sight? Had Reginald Davis eaten a Black Sea Bass pulled from the murky waters, leading to a strange illness and his eventual disappearance? Did the black sands come alive one predawn morning and envelop Old Gus Warner as he loaded his fishing boat, stealing him away to unknown shores? I didn’t know. These people had surely vanished from the area, but the details were no more than quietly whispered gossip among the locals. Residents of Delhyme loved to talk, but nothing they said struck true. They were simply extensions of the ghost stories that drew me there in the first place. When I asked questions, tried to find the primary sources of the stories, I’d invariably hit a dead end. That is, until I met Benny Dickson. Another outsider like me. Someone who saw past the hastily laid camouflage and poorly executed subterfuge. Someone who had answers. # While Vanessa played with the surgical tools, I set up the staging area in my living room, placing yards of plastic sheeting. In the middle, I placed a wood chair sourced from Delhyme, made by a carpenter using local oak. It was sturdy and only looked better with age and staining. “You almost ready?” She stomped a foot like an adolescent waiting for a promised ice cream cone. “Almost.” I removed the lid to the container. A strong smell of sea water and fertilizer escaped along with a gurgling squeal. “Bring me a scalpel.” This time the squeal was one of excitement issued by Vanessa. She rummaged through the tools and jogged over to the counter. Vanessa jabbed the blade at me. “Now?” “Whoa, careful.” I held my hand out. Vanessa frowned theatrically and handed me the scalpel. “What is that?” She leaned over the container and waved a hand in front of her nose. “Gross. Smells like rotting fish heads.” “Can you tell what’s water and what isn’t?” Holding her nose, she inspected what looked like undulating liquid. “Um.” “Check this out.” I poked the surface with the handle of the scalpel, withdrawing quickly. The creature in the water became visible as it flashed blue and red lights along thin tendrils connected to a bulbous body. Tiny groups of spheres, like miniature grape clusters, hung from floating tentacles the size of threads. Other tubular protrusions connected to a set of wings. “What—?” “Bioluminescence,” I said. Vanessa dropped her hand. “But what is it?” She turned, eyes wide. “And why do we have it?” “You said on your profile that you like to play nurse.” I handed the scalpel back to her. “And I need an assistant.” # Summer on a college campus can be quiet, even eerie. But Eskuatep was something else. One hot morning, I walked to campus, intending to explore the cove. The builders of Eskuatep managed to completely block access except by walking through campus. The walkways were made of the same white stone I’d seen around town. The same as buildings, or at least similar to my untrained eyes. Ponderous trees and hedges grew along the paths, fencing them in and making navigation difficult. Harder still due to lack of signs or maps. It was impossible to stay on a single path, as the walkway forked and forked again. A tall building loomed ahead. More tower than schoolhouse. One leg of the path led to the front of the building, but there were no doors. I walked clockwise, to the adjoining wall and found only vine-covered stone. A dizziness overcame me as I continued to circle, finding the third wall as bare as the first. No doors. No windows for that matter. I leaned on my knees, exhausted for no reason. My head buzzed with summer sounds. The coastal scents became oppressive. I turned until I saw a break in the trees where the coast must be, then headed in that direction. But the paths did not cooperate. With the overgrown vegetation on either side, it was like being trapped in a hedge maze. The pathways zigged and zagged off course, branching into various directions, none of which suited me. I heard footsteps behind me, echoing as if in a long hallway. I saw no one, but increased my speed, nonetheless. I chose a path. Then another. I should have wanted to find the person, ask them how in God’s name to escape the labyrinth. But my gut told me no . My gut told me to run . And once the slippery word got inside, with terror mounting, my body reacted independent of logic. The wind blew a rancid stench of rotting fish, so concentrated my eyes teared. Footsteps grew louder behind me. My mind screamed to run faster, and I did. I ran until I saw a tall building with no doors. I kept running until I passed a familiar set of paths, and another unmarked building. How big was this campus? Why did the monolithic buildings defy reason? I stopped and checked the skyline. The coast was behind me. Footsteps continued. In a fit of panic, I jumped headfirst through a hedge. Thorns drew thin lines of blood across my face and arms until I tumbled into a stand of oak and pine. Free of the dreaded stone pathway, my head cleared of the insistent buzzing, and Forgotten Cove presented itself. I regained my feet and stumbled through the woods until I hit sand, then continued until the sand turned dark with seawater. I fell to my knees and a wave soaked my pants. Laughter bubbled through the exhaustion, fear, and confusion. I gripped big handfuls of wet sand, trying to ground myself in the reality of land and sea. The breeze was cool again. The buzzing only that of summertime insects. In a fit of joy, I stood and tore off my shirt, the delicious sea calling to me. Faint blue and red lights sparkled in the crystalline water. I kicked off my shoes and removed my pants. Broken shells bit my feet. Waves licked my ankles, then my shins. The teasing tide inflamed my desire to be fully immersed and protected from impudent sun. Behind me, a voice halted my progress. “I wouldn’t do that if I were you.” The owner of the pursuing footsteps had found me. # Vanessa watched curiously as I disrobed, removing my shirt, pants, shoes, and socks. I bared all of my crosshatched scars. Countless cuts and countless screams. She bit her lip, my scarred body a promise. “What about your underwear?” “Not necessary.” Her expression collapsed. She eyed the container that again appeared as if it only held water. Then she looked at the chest of surgical tools, frowning. “Listen—” “I need your help with this.” “Help with what ? Are we going to fuck?” “No.” “Then what the hell were you doing on a dating site?” “A fetish… extreme fetish dating site.” “What’s your point?” She threw her hands in the air. “I’m not screwing a sea monster, man.” “That’s not—” “Then what? Spill it or I’m gone. And I’m taking my too spicy bag of food.” “Okay.” I held my hands up in surrender. “Give me five minutes. I’ll explain, and then if you want to leave, you can leave. Food and all.” I carried the container to the wood chair and sat. “Deal?” Vanessa looked around my place, maybe thinking she could squeeze me for some cash if she stayed. Maybe just curious about what I had in mind. She retrieved her food from the counter and sat on a stool. Digging into the bag, she said, “You’re lucky I’m hungry.” # I turned to face my pursuer. The stocky man wearing forest green chest waders placed a metal bucket onto the sand. “Step out of the water.” “Why are you following me?” I backed up. My state of undress made me feel vulnerable, but the tone of the man’s voice twisted that vulnerability into all-out fear. “Please. You need to step out of the—” “Ouch!” A searing pain shot through my leg, as if a molten thread of sugar had dripped on me. The man’s eyes filled with fear. He grabbed my arm and pulled me toward shore. I resisted at first, but another line of fire ignited my leg. Then another, and another, until my vision went dull and gray. I couldn’t hear the man anymore. His words were a distorted warbling. The water and sand turned to slime, oozing between my toes, coating my feet with an infected bisque. Traces of fire extended to my thighs, winding around my muscles and tendons, like being bound by barbed wire. Summer sounds buzzed impatient and aggressive. I stumbled and the man tried to hold on to me, but his hands were barely there. The cove, the sky, the world was barely there. A multitude of disparate shapes and colors took its place. Things with no context. My mind attempted to make sense of what I saw and regurgitated an aneurysm of words. A gurgling range of ship-ended wafers. Coagulated sonorous beacons attempting flight. Grim drapery of fleshy wings dripped from the sky. Atomized extensions of brief granite took a stumbling step skyward. Every breath was a nightmare. I inhaled liquid fire. My skin bubbled in clusters of melting fat, basting in my own juices. I screamed inhuman. My ears popped and warm liquid poured out, dripping over my jawline. The next scream was terrible silence. Something I couldn’t see tugged my arm and I fell to all fours on a rubber ground crawling with biting things. I had no name for the flat corpuscular monsters. Something tugged at my legs. I collapsed onto my stomach and the ravenous creatures feasted. I rolled in an attempt to dislodge them but fell motionless and utterly dumbstruck. A sharp pain shot through my leg. My new hell flickered. Another cut and Forgotten Cove returned. The man who’d been following me was crouched at my bleeding legs with a knife in one gloved hand and a cluster of translucent, rubbery tendrils in the other. A quivering mass of blubber lay on the sand, a multitude of clustered protrusions convulsing in an epileptic jitterbug. I kicked away, sand flying around me, the sky spinning. “Stay put!” The man dropped the remains of the creature onto the sand and grabbed his metal bucket. He rushed over and thrust it into my hands. He winked. “I’m Benny.” I replied by heaving my guts into the container. A great gushing flood of chunky black and green ichor poured from me. It tasted of acidic sea. Like metallic rot. The torrent continued into absurdity, filling the large bucket with thick sludge. When I finally finished, Benny smiled and said, “Nice to meet you to.” Wiping my mouth, I stared at what had come out of me with disgust. I spit a few times into the sand before I was able to speak, and my first words were about as eloquent as I could muster. “What the fuck? Benny retrieved what looked like a huge digital thermometer and placed it into my vomit. “Ph Meter,” he said as if the explanation would make sense. He checked the readout. “Primo stuff.” I had gained a bit of composure and was able to expand on my previous inquiry. “What the actual fuck ?” He scooted next to me and sat on the dry sand, looking at the waters of Forgotten Cove. We sat at the bottom of a shoehorn, the land protruding out on both sides to create a sheltered bay. “I know it didn’t much taste like it, but that stuff is ambrosia. Nectar of the Gods.” “It’s vomit.” He laughed as if my response was deadpan wit and not utter confusion. “Sure is.” I spit again, unable to get the taste from my mouth. My legs were bleeding and there were angry welts, but none of the other injuries I’d experienced in my trance were visible. Had they even happened? “Sorry about that. I had to cut I Mávri Pórta from you before you discorporated.” He rubbed a sandy hand over his dark shag of hair. “That’s what I call the siphonophores in the cove. Means the black door . It’s Greek.” My head spun, my mouth tasted like sea rot, and the sun was baking me alive. “Siphonophores?” He reached into a big pocket of his waders and handed me a bottle of water. I grabbed it and guzzled half before he had a chance to respond. “You know the Portuguese man o' war?” “The jellyfish?” “Actually a siphonophore.” He spread his hands. “A super organism. Made up of thousands of microscopic organisms. Zooids. Kind of like different cells in the body, they each have a job. Locomotion, reproduction, digestion. Get it?” “Fine, yeah. But—” “Yeah, yeah. I know. But what the actual fuck?” He rubbed his hair again, transplanting even more sand to his scalp. “You know the stories? About Eskuatep? Delhyme?” My heart sped. “The disappearances you mean. Yeah, I’ve heard.” He nodded, still looking across the water. “Well. Now you’ve seen too.” He turned to me, his eyes serious and unblinking. “You’ve seen where the missing go .” # I explained this all to Vanessa as she ate the blackened chicken over rice. “Bullshit.” “It’s the truth.” Vanessa got up and peered into the water. She didn’t even wrinkle her nose at the smell that time. When her eyes found mine, they held a glint of curiosity. “What do you want me to do?” She still wanted to be convinced. “Simple.” I retrieved a four-quart Cambro food container and placed it at the foot of the chair, next to I Mávri Pórta. “ I’m going to let the nematocysts sting me.” “Nemo—?” “Never mind. Once I put my hand into the water, give it exactly one minute.” I pointed at my coffee table. “There’s a stopwatch over there. Use that. You’ll also need a scalpel, knife, whatever you want to use. After exactly one minute, you need to cut this thing off me.” Her mouth twitched into a tentative smile. “Yeah?” “Yes. Cut me . I’m confident you know how to do that without killing me because, well...” She spun her bowie knife with a flourish. “That answers why me ?” “Found my last assistant on the same fetish site.” “Why not hire a doctor or nurse?” “Tried. Surprisingly, they had some ethical issues with the whole thing. Either that or they thought it was a joke.” “You know, I’m not so sure I believe you either.” “Maybe. But you’re curious. Curious enough to let this play out.” “What can I say? You intrigue me.” She started toward me with her knife. “One last thing. Make sure to put on the big rubber gloves. You don’t want this thing touching you.” “Right.” She retrieved all the equipment, pulled the gloves on, and took her position in front of me. “Ready?” “Set.” She smiled and hit the stopwatch. “Go!” # The ichor I heaved out of me. That chunky green and black ooze. It was the key to everything. Benny wasn’t supposed to be on campus over the summer. No one was. They had all gone abroad for sabbatical. But Benny saw his chance for something more. He wasn’t actually a student at Eskuatep University. He was a genius, that was true. But he wasn’t rich. That second all-important prerequisite to become part of the elite pantheon of Eskuatep alumni. “I wriggled my way in,” he explained on the hot sand of the beach. “A janitor first. They kept me on the outside for a long time. Still do.” “But you were in.” “I was in,” He agreed. “And I saw what they were doing with the Siphonophores. How they used them. How they exploited them.” I looked over my shoulder at the strange, abandoned campus. “Where are they?” “Gathering knowledge. Gaining power.” # I plunged my hand into the slimy water and I Mávri Pórta lit up like a dread Christmas tree. Thread-like tendrils wrapped around my wrist and I imagined the thousands of nematocysts expelling like microscopic harpoon guns, barbed threads exploding toxins into my body. I screamed. No matter how many times I did this, I always screamed. Before my eyes rolled back, I saw a peculiar expression cross Vanessa’s face. As she stepped closer, watching my terror, a sexual hunger consumed her features. The smell of Forgotten Cove enveloped me, and for a second, I saw Benny as he was that day on the shore, explaining the lost mysteries of Eskuatep to me. The way they’d constructed the pathways to confuse outsiders. The hidden doorways into buildings. I saw myself as I was too. Younger, hopeful, curious. Looking for answers to an unsolved mystery. Not aware of the agony of discovery. Then the world fell away as I, for the hundredth time, glimpsed oblivion. Snakes of linked tendons slithered around me like a tornado of flesh. I smelled toasted marshmallow. The horizon boasted three suns, heat so intense it was ice against my skin. Time meant nothing in that space between places. There were occasions I thought I’d been lost and trapped, hours passing, before one minute expired in my world. Other times my journey was a millisecond blip. So when my apartment flickered, superimposed over the surreal space I inhabited, I thought nothing of it. Until another heat began to build. A different kind of heat. The apartment came back again, and this time Vanessa’s ghost was riding me, bare chested and moaning. Her face twisted with pleasure. She held the back of my neck as she bucked. With her free hand, she filleted my arm. My living room disappeared, and I was floating in a multicolored miasma, surrounded by bulbous-eyed humanoids. The shock of their staring orbs caused me to gasp, sucking gelatin into my lungs. Then my eyes shot wide. The apartment returned, still only a transparent veil, but enough so I could breathe. Vanessa groaned, barely aware of her surroundings. Barely aware that I Mávri Pórta was freeing me from its grasp as she continued to cut and slice my arm. “Get it off!” Her eyes opened lazily, the smile of satisfaction loathe to flee. But she heard me. “Tear it off!” “Almost. There.” She covered my mouth with her hand. Oh, dear Christ. She covered my mouth with her bare hand. Where were the gloves? My words were lost behind her hand and between worlds as I tried to warn her. A golden orb appeared beneath my feet. I fell from a great height, waves of blue light swirling around me, the golden ground racing up. I threw my hands out bracing for impact, but when it came, I was on the plastic-covered floor of my apartment. She’d done it, she’d— I grabbed the Cambro and released the pent-up pressure inside, a flood of thick, living fluid, studded with gems of insight. The nectar of the gods. Ambrosia. Wiping my mouth, I looked to retrieve the sea creature. If it was still alive, we could preserve it for another trip. “He— I— Whe—” Vanessa was on the floor, her red hair spread around her like a puddle of blood. I Mávri Pórta was wrapped around her face and neck, tendrils extending over her naked body like spider webs. “No!” I ran to the chest and retrieved two scalpels and gloves. Vanessa faded completely out of sight, then pulsed back. I grabbed her arm to stabilize her and cut deep. Maybe too deep, but she was covered with the super organism’s filaments. I sliced again and blood spurt me in the face. I kept on cutting as Vanessa groaned and wriggled and screamed. The whole apartment stunk of sea and sex. My mouth was gritty. My vision was blurry from the trip. Blood was everywhere. Vanessa convulsed and my grip slipped. Then she slipped. Right out of our reality. Out of our world. I Mávri Pórta fell to the floor, squirming and satiated. Vanessa was gone. I fell back, panting, wiping blood from my face. “Not again.” # I didn’t sleep. There was too much clean up. Besides, I could never rest after conferring with I Mávri Pórta. Too many strange thoughts would swirl around my mind, keeping me up. I mopped blood and slime instead. At dawn I drove to The Black Door and met with Benny Dickson. The place was empty at that hour expect for prep cooks and Gregory. My waiter brought me to Benny’s office. He glanced at the filled Cambro and smiled. “This is good.” Benny sat behind his clean desk, hands folded under his chin. “I’m glad you had a change of heart, Sam. I really am.” I placed the Cambro on his desk. He tapped the lid. “All for me?” “I owed you.” Frowning, he said, “You did.” He licked his lips, drummed his fingers on the edge of the container. “You won’t try to quit again. Won’t try to keep the ambrosia for yourself.” This was not a question. I laughed a single, dry huff. Quit. Like it was a job. Like I had a choice. “No.” “How was the girl I got you?” My throat tightened. “She didn’t listen.” Benny ran his finger along the edge of the container, his eyes fixed on the lumpy, contents. “That’s your fuck up. Don’t blame me if you don’t prep them properly.” “Yeah.” My guts twisted. He wasn’t wrong, but I was on the clock. “My fault.” Benny stood and peered into the green-black filth. He always tested the product. He couldn’t help himself. “Go ahead.” A faint expression of suspicion crossed his features, but he was already on the hook. He dipped his hand into the muck, searching for the crystalline orbs. “Cheers.” The ambrosia flashed red and blue. Benny gasped and tried to pull his hand away, but it was too late. His screams filled the office as I Mávri Pórta wrapped around his hand and arm. He bucked and fell into the chair, taking the siphonophore with him. It wriggled along his chest, the float bladder quivering with movement, the rubbery wings pulsating, the tendrils flashing hunter lights and injecting toxins. “What did you do?” His voice was already half gone, echoing from another place. “Freedom comes at a price. It’s time for you to see Hell for yourself.” “You son of a—” He flickered and I saw his veins and tendons. His ocular cavities and bones. I didn’t care. I was more scar tissue than man by then, and this was the only way out. “Bye, Benny.” He came back long enough for me to see the terror in his eyes. This man who’d saved me only to enslave me. I was done being a guinea pig. Done being afraid. But as Benny disappeared from the world, I realized there was one thing I wasn’t done with. I was an explorer. A journeyman. And finally, I had the key to the black door all to myself.
